name: About Bagels-- General Directions 3 A
recipeIngredient: 12 cups bread flour, plus extra for dusting
Dried fruits, nuts, vegetables, seeds, spices, chocolate, or any combination of flavorings
recipeInstructions:
STEP 2: SHAPE BAGELS
Prepare baking sheets by lightly greasing them with nonstick vegetable spray or oil using your fingertips or waxed paper.
Reach into the bread machine pan and pull dough out. If it is slightly sticky, dip your fingers in flour first.
Some machines punch dough down automatically at the end of the rise cycle, which is usually adequate to remove gases. Otherwise, punch dough down manually.
Knead dough once or twice and let rest for 5 minutes. If the dough is still too wet and sticky, lightly dust the bread board or your hands with flour and knead until it has a smooth, elastic consistency. Bagel dough should be stiff but elastic; if it's too stiff, sprinkle water on it or moisten your hands and knead in moisture.
Roll and pull dough into a rectangle about 10x14 inches for a 1-pound recipe and 14x18 inches for a 1 1/2-pound recipe. Let rest for 5 minutes.
Sprinkle with desired dried fruits, nuts, vegetables, seeds, spices, chocolate, or any combination of flavorings. Roll dough into a log and knead the ingredients in for a minute or so.
The dough should weigh slightly more than your recipe size. Divide dough into pieces based on bagel size you want. A 1 1/2-pound recipe yields 8 to 12 finished bagels, each weighing 2 to 3 ounces and measuring about 4 inches across. Use a food scale for consistency or measure with a ruler. Cut smaller pieces for mini bagels.
Knead added ingredients well before shaping each bagel. You can also divide dough and add different ingredients to each portion to get varied flavors in one batch of bagels.
CONTINUED IN ABOUT BAGELS -- GENERAL DIRECTIONS 3 B
The Best Bagels are made at home by Dona Z. Meilach, ISBN 1-55867-131-5
Carolyn Shaw, April 1996 From: Home Cook
